entries.content:
That was nothing at all—like a spark landing on a polished kitchen counter: barely a prickle, if anything, and certainly not enough to muss my hair or frighten the cat. I do suppose I’m expected to act as if I’ve felt something the way a dutiful wife might swoon decorously when her husband brags about his strength—“Oh, you brute!”—but there’s no need to pretend just yet. I am watching you, though, with a hostess’s nervous half-smile, waiting to see which petty cruelty comes next, because I know what happens to women who complain too early. Isn’t it just like a good homemaker to accept a discomfort quietly, smothering annoyance so thoroughly it might as well be a layer of dust behind the settee? But one can’t help but think about how training for obedience starts innocently, with a “little nothing.” Still, my manners hold, and I won’t give you the satisfaction of drama—just a gentle, bracing readiness to continue, as any tidy soul would.
